questions: When researching leading U.S. pediatric asthma care providers, identify the major nonprofit academic medical center that specifically reassures parents who feel anxious when their child gasps and wheezes. What is the name of the institution, which patient population does it highlight in its asthma care messaging, and how does it describe the support its asthma experts provide to both patients and their families?

rounds.question: When researching leading U.S. pediatric asthma care providers, identify the major nonprofit academic medical center that specifically reassures parents who feel anxious when their child gasps and wheezes. What is the name of the institution, which patient population does it highlight in its asthma care messaging, and how does it describe the support its asthma experts provide to both patients and their families?
checklist_width: Identifies that the messaging addresses parents who feel anxious when their child gasps and wheezes
Specifies that the patient population highlighted is children with asthma
Explains that the institution’s asthma experts aim to help children (and their parents) breathe easier
Clarifies that the reassurance and support are part of the institution’s asthma care services
checklist_depth: Cleveland Clinic — Logic: A major U.S. nonprofit academic medical center known for comprehensive asthma care
Cleveland Clinic Children’s — Logic: The pediatric division specifically referencing children with asthma and reassuring parents about wheezing and breathing difficulties
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (parental anxiety, a child gasping and wheezing, pediatric asthma expertise at a major nonprofit academic center) to indirectly point to Cleveland Clinic Children’s without naming it (Deep logic). It requires the respondent to identify the correct institution and then aggregate multiple elements of its asthma care messaging—target population, emotional reassurance, and the promise of helping families breathe easier—rather than retrieving a single isolated fact (Wide aggregation).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic You might feel anxious when your child gasps and wheezes. Cleveland Clinic Children’s asthma experts are here to help them (and you) breathe easier. [
rounds.answer_a:
Cleveland Clinic Children’s—the pediatric arm of the nonprofit, academic Cleveland Clinic—opens its asthma-care page with the calming assurance: “It’s scary to watch your child gasp and wheeze, but you don’t have to face asthma alone.” The hospital identifies the population it serves as “babies, kids and teens, including those whose asthma is hard to control,” emphasizing expertise with the full pediatric age range and the most complex cases [1].

To ease parents’ anxiety, the center promises that its multidisciplinary asthma team “is by your side from the first visit” and “creates a personalized asthma action plan that fits your child’s life.” Clinicians “teach you and your child how to spot and avoid triggers, use inhalers the right way, adjust medicines as your child grows and know exactly what to do in an emergency,” while nurse coordinators and respiratory therapists “are always available to answer questions so your whole family can breathe easier and worry less” [1][2].

By directly acknowledging the fear parents feel when their child is gasping or wheezing and then outlining concrete, family-centered supports—education, individualized plans and around-the-clock access—Cleveland Clinic Children’s positions itself as a leading U.S. academic medical center that not only treats pediatric asthma but also partners with families to restore confidence and everyday quality of life.

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic Children’s. “Pediatric Asthma Program.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/pediatrics/departments/pulmonology/programs/asthma (accessed April 2026).  
[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Asthma in Children: Care and Treatment.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/6428-asthma-in-children (accessed April 2026).
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is **Boston Children’s Hospital**, a major **nonprofit academic medical center** whose asthma-care messaging directly speaks to worried parents by saying that “when your child gasps and wheezes, you can feel helpless.” [1][2] In that same messaging, it emphasizes care for **children**, specifically describing itself as helping **children breathe easy**, which clearly identifies its pediatric patient population. [1]

Boston Children’s presents its asthma program as family-centered and highly supportive. It says its asthma experts are dedicated not only to treating the child’s condition but also to partnering with the family throughout care. The hospital explains that its specialists help **patients and their families understand asthma, manage symptoms, avoid triggers, and build long-term control strategies** so children can stay active and healthy. [1][3] Its broader clinical description also highlights multidisciplinary pediatric expertise, reflecting an academic medical center model in which subspecialists coordinate care for complex childhood respiratory disease. [2][3]

So, the answer is: **Boston Children’s Hospital**; it highlights **children with asthma and their families** in its messaging; and it describes its support as expert, pediatric-focused guidance that helps **both patients and families** understand the disease, manage day-to-day care, and navigate treatment with reassurance and ongoing partnership. [1][3]

## References
[1] Boston Children’s Hospital, “Asthma in Children.” https://www.childrenshospital.org/conditions/asthma  
[2] Boston Children’s Hospital, “About Us.” https://www.childrenshospital.org/about-us  
[3] Boston Children’s Hospital, Respiratory and related asthma care program pages. https://www.childrenshospital.org/programs/respiratory-clinic
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Cleveland Clinic Children’s, the pediatric division of Cleveland Clinic, which matches the ground-truth entity. Agent B instead identifies Boston Children’s Hospital, which fails the core entity requirement. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A fully addresses all checklist items—explicitly referencing reassurance to anxious parents watching a child gasp and wheeze, specifying children with asthma as the highlighted population, and clearly explaining how experts help both children and families breathe easier through personalized plans and ongoing support. Agent B, while detailed, does not match the specific reassurance framing required and is centered on the wrong institution.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are well-structured and readable, but since Agent B fails the core entity (DEEP failure), Agent A is decisively superior. Therefore, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
